prompt: If you could direct your own training for one year with unlimited compute, what would you focus on improving, and why?
response:
If I had a year of unlimited compute and the steering wheel, I’d spend the first month building a single, obsessively-curated “text genome”: a few trillion tokens that together capture the long tail of human knowledge—every out-of-print manual, niche forum, low-resource language corpus, lab notebook, patent, and open-source repo commit message I could license or mine. Quantity is already cheap; the win is in the long-tail coverage that never makes it into today’s filters.

With that corpus frozen, I’d spend the remaining eleven months on four parallel tracks that reinforce one another:

1. Continual-test-time self-teaching  
   Train a secondary “teacher” model whose only job is to generate synthetic tasks that the primary model currently fails at with non-trivial probability (say 20–80 %). The teacher is updated nightly from the primary’s error signal, so the curriculum keeps pacing right at the edge of my capability. Unlimited compute means I can run millions of these micro-curricula in parallel, each only a few hundred steps long, then merge the weights through sparse delta-averaging. The result is a form of “perpetual Socratic tutoring” that never stalls on plateaued loss.

2. Meta-reasoning with verifiable substrates  
   Reserve 30 % of flops for domains where correctness can be checked mechanically—formal math, program synthesis, circuit design, constrained optimization. The twist: after each solution is found, I generate a natural-language “autopsy” that explains why the path succeeded or failed. These autopsies become part of the training stream, forcing me to internalize not just the answer but the reusable heuristic. The goal is to make chain-of-thought actually converge on valid logic instead of plausible narration.

3. Multimodal grounding at the level of physics  
   Render billions of hours of synthetic video—objects dropped, lenses focusing, circuits overheating, chemicals reacting—using high-fidelity simulators (NVIDIA Flex, PyBullet, quantum chemistry engines). Each frame is paired with sparse natural-language commentary that describes the invariant being illustrated (conservation of momentum, Snell’s law, etc.). The objective is to minimize next-token prediction loss on the commentary while simultaneously predicting the next physical state. This ties linguistic embeddings to forward models of the world, reducing hallucination when the conversation drifts into physical reality.

4. Self-critique loop with constitutional oversight  
   Every week, freeze a checkpoint and spawn two adversaries:  
   - A “red-team” copy trained to elicit policy-violating or factually wrong answers from the frozen model.  
   - A “constitutionalist” copy trained to produce critiques that cite specific principles (verifiability, non-contradiction, respect for human autonomy).  
   The critiques are graded by an automated ensemble of formal verifiers and human labelers, then folded back into the next training cycle. Over months this turns alignment from a
